title: An Investigation into the nature of psychological resilience in junior athletes
authors: Hollie Fountain
venue: Edinburgh Napier Research Repository (Edinburgh Napier University)
abstract: Psychological resilience has been described as a multidimensional, context specific concept, and has been defined in numerous ways that attempt to encapsulate the process by which individuals positively adapt following stress or significant adversity. Research within competitive sport has highlighted several components that influence this process, which include; meta-cognitions and challenge appraisals, coping strategies, personal risk and protective factors, and sociocultural influences (Brown et al., 2015; Galli & Vealey, 2008; Fletcher & Sarkar, 2012; Sarkar & Fletcher, 2014a). Significantly, resilience is described as a dynamic process that is developed through exposure to challenge within the competitive environment (Galli & Vealey, 2008); however, little is known about the nature of psychological resilience at a junior level. The understanding of how resilience is conceptualised at this level is important as this knowledge can help to foster the appropriate protective and promotive factors required to thrive in a competitive junior environment, and best equip athletes for future periods of unrest. The aims of the current research program were to investigate the nature of psychological resilience within a junior sport context, and to explore appropriate measures or methodological approaches by which to achieve this. To achieve these, eight research objectives are presented. To address these objectives, five research investigations were proposed: Study 1. This study aimed to explore the psychometric qualities of the original 25-item CD-RISC (Connor & Davidson, 2003) amongst a sample of junior athletes. Three hundred and forty seven athletes (M age=15.42, SD=1.72) completed the original CD-RISC questionnaire. Participants represented a range of individual and team sports. Internal consistency and factor structure were analysed using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) and exploratory factor analyses (EFA). CFAs did not support the original 5-factor or unitary factor structure of the 25-item CD-RISC, but did support a unidimensional shortened 10-item measure (Cambell-Sills & Stein, 2007). Subsequently, an EFA and CFA also supported a valid and reliable 2-factor sport specific version of the CD-RISC, which was favoured based on stronger conceptual and theoretical support. This study supports the contention that resilience is not consistent across all populations and context specific measures may be required e.g., sport specific. The emergent 2-factor measurement model suggests an underlying structure of resilience in sport that represents an individual’s control through adversity and growth mindset. Study 2. The aim of this study was to explore the nature of resilience within junior sport, with a specific focus on sport type, gender and age differences, and the association between resilience and sensation seeking characteristics. Participants completed the modified version of the CD-RISC, which emerged in the previous study and the Brief Sensation Seeking Scale (BSSS; Hoyle at al., 2002), which measures dispositional risk taking behaviours. The results suggested that male and team athletes have significantly higher resilience scores than their female and individual sport counterparts. In general, protective factors associated with resilience positively relate to sensation seeking characteristics. Specifically, feelings relating to ‘control through adversity’ more broadly relate to tendencies leading to greater risk exposure. These findings may suggest that those with a greater perception of control take more calculated risks and set goals that are more challenging. This may offer the opportunity to increase personal mastery through developed interpersonal relations, emotional expression, problem solving skills and coping resources. Nevertheless, our understanding of resilience seems limited by the capacity of a psychometric questionnaire to encapsulate such a complex construct. Study 3. This study aimed to provide a review of the literature concerning resilience in athletes, with a specific focus on identifying the differing methodological approaches to examine the nature of the construct in sport. Fourteen research articles that attempted to directly measure psychological resilience with an athlete sample were identified using both quantitative (n=8) and qualitative (n=6) approaches. Quantitative research has increased conceptual understanding of resilience in sport, relating to its positive associations with similar constructs (e.g., mental toughness), and its moderation qualities. This approach permits statistical analyses to track development, however is unlikely to offer sufficient depth to understanding given the complexities surrounding both the construct of psychological resilience and the nature of an elite sporting environment. Qualitative studies have helped to develop theoretical understanding of psychological resilience amongst athletes through adopting phenomenological methodologies, however, the application of knowledge relies on user generalisability alone and does not offer an objective measure of the construct. The review proposes an exploration of novel methodological approaches that consider the positive elements of both qualitative and quantitative research, but does not consolidate their pitfalls. Study 4. The purpose of this study was to develop a novel tool to measure psychological resilience using a Q-method approach. Specifically, this study aimed to construct a Q-set, by identifying the subjective viewpoints of junior rugby league players, associated with how they would respond to stress or adversity and their perceptions of the resilience process. Twenty-nine junior rugby league players (aged 13-14) were recruited to take part in one of two focus groups designed to generate statements relating to responses to adversity. Thirty statements emerged following inductive thematic analysis, and were retained for the Q-set. There are commonalities between these statements and characteristics of theoretical models and previous research concerning psychological resilience in sport. Study 5. The purpose of this study was to use the Q-set developed in the previous study to explore the nature of psychological resilience in the context of junior Rugby League, using a novel Q-sort method. Sixty junior rugby league players (aged 13-14) completed a standard Q-sort protocol, ranking the previously developed 30-item Q-set using a fixed quasi normal distribution, with anchors of +5 (most like me) to -5 (least like me). PQ Method statistical analysis software was used to analyse the data. Principle component analysis with varimax rotation identified four distinct subgroups that explained 72% of the total variance. These groups were distinguished through patterns relating to: social support, emotional control, unpleasant emotions, personal resources, and cognitive strategies. Shared qualities across the four subgroups were also identified, and included low ratings for evasion strategies, and seeking support, whilst generally high ratings for perseverance. The results from this study showed that junior rugby league players display a range of psychological responses when experiencing adversity and four subgroups with both defining and shared characteristics emerged. This study provides preliminary evidence for the potential usefulness of a Q-method approach for understanding the process of resilience in junior sport. Q-methodology provides an alternative to previous research designs attempting to understand the nature of resilience, and offers an engaging activity to participants, encouraging analytical reflections of their experiences. In summary, the data collected within the current research program has presented an original contribution to knowledge concerning the nature of psychological resilience in junior sport. The thesis has delivered the first study of its kind, by employing Q-methodology to understand psychological resilience, revealing previously untapped complexities associated with the construct. This approach offers future researchers and practitioners the depth of insight and level of objectivity associated with qualitative and quantitative measures respectively, and recommends this as a viable alternative to psychometric measures of resilience.
affiliations: Edinburgh Napier University

title: AI in Cybersecurity: A Systematic Review and Conceptual Audit Model
authors: Ndaedzo Rananga; H.S Venter
venue: Journal of Information Systems and Informatics
abstract: Technological advances, particularly in Artificial Intelligence (AI), are accelerating digital transformation while increasing system complexity and exposure to sophisticated cyber threats. These developments challenge traditional cybersecurity audit approaches, which are largely periodic, retrospective, and focused on binary control checks. In response, the adoption of generative AI (GenAI) and predictive AI (PredAI) in cybersecurity auditing is becoming increasingly important. Although AI can improve audit intelligence, scalability, timeliness, and effectiveness, its use also raises concerns about transparency, governance, and auditor independence. This study employed a two-stage methodology. First, a systematic literature review following PRISMA examined studies published between 2021 and 2026, yielding 36 eligible articles. The review found that hybrid AI approaches dominate the literature (58.3%), followed by GenAI (25.0%) and PredAI (16.7%). Despite this growing interest, the literature gives limited attention to risk-based auditing approaches that move beyond binary control confirmation toward context-aware, intelligence-driven cyber risk assessment. Second, using Design Science Research, the study developed the conceptual Anti-Sheriff cybersecurity auditing model. The model shifts auditing from compliance-driven enforcement to intelligence-supported risk governance, enabling continuous auditing, better risk prioritisation, and stronger organisational cyber resilience.
affiliations: University of Pretoria

title: Experience using artificial intelligence in the digital transformation of education: benefits and challenges
authors: Arturs Medveckis; Tamara Pigozne; Rita Birzina; Ivita Pelnena
abstract: Aim The aim of the research is to analyse teachers and adult educators’ experiences of the application of artificial intelligence in the digital transformation of education. Methods In the study the quantitative data collection method—a questionnaire—“International Survey on Artificial Intelligence in Higher Education, Training and Adult Learning” and data processing methods for secondary data collection—descriptive statistics (Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation), and the Mann–Whitney test to determine the statistical significance of differences between two independent target groups (teachers and adult educators) have been applied. Results The research sample consisted of representatives of educational institutions of Latvia—34 teachers and 83 adult educators. The descriptive statistics and Mann–Whitney test results show that both teachers and adult educators similarly assess the impact of AI application on performance and work efficiency and productivity, decision-making, problem-solving, awareness formation and interdisciplinary concept application skills, mental health, learning outcomes and challenges related to AI use ( p ≥ 0.05). Teachers, compared to adult educators, have a higher opinion of the application of artificial intelligence for work purposes. Adult educators have a higher opinion of the impact of AI on the development of learners’ awareness formation skills, learners’ employment and their work performance, and physical and social health ( p ≤ 0.05). Conclusion AI is a new global reality that opens up new paths for knowledge acquisition, whereas the social environment is also facing new challenges. AI tools can be used by a wide range of users who have prior knowledge and skills in constantly changing IT application. Despite the inertia of the education system and the length of bureaucratized decision-making, proactive action is needed that would balance the technological development with the acquisition of new knowledge and skills based on high moral standards at all levels of education, involving high-tech implementers and cooperating with educational staff, scientists and other social partners. A descriptive cross-sectional study design has been chosen for the study and the instrument applied is the survey “International Survey on Artificial Intelligence in Higher Education, Training and Adult Learning” developed by the Singapore Institute of Adult Education within the framework of the 3rd network “Professionalization of Adult Educators in ASEM Countries” of the Asia-Europe Lifelong Learning and Education Research (ASEM LLL Hub).
affiliations: University of Liepāja; University of Latvia; Latvia University of Life Sciences and Technologies

title: Versatile Night/Day Framework for Longitudinal Pi-based Imaging (VeNFLoPi): Open-source Camera Setup For Behavioral Assays
authors: Tara Delorme; Mackenzie Gamble; Ryan Logan
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
abstract: Abstract Quantitative behavioral analysis increasingly relies on video-based approaches combined with machine-learning tools to capture posture, locomotion, and complex behaviors that cannot be easily quantified manually. Commercial camera systems are often expensive, inflexible, and difficult to scale, while existing open-source solutions can be limited in low-light performance, multi-camera integration, and long-term stability. Here, we present VeNFLoPi (Versatile Night/Day Framework for Longitudinal Pi-based Imaging), an open-source Raspberry Pi-based video acquisition system optimized for longitudinal behavioral recordings across light and dark conditions. VeNFLoPi integrates Raspberry Pi cameras, custom infrared illumination, and centralized recording through Open Broadcaster Software (OBS), enabling real-time monitoring, high-resolution and high-frame-rate acquisition, robust file management, and automated local or cloud storage. Flexible 3D-printed mounts allow cameras and IR lighting to be positioned for top-down or angled views in compact behavioral chambers, with wide-angle lenses and fisheye correction ensuring distortion-free recording. The system is plug-and-play, requires no custom coding, and produces video formats immediately compatible with machine-learning workflows such as DeepLabCut, LabGym, and other pipelines. Optional scripts allow timestamping, preprocessing, and stitching of long-duration recordings. A full setup guide and system image are provided, making VeNFLoPi a flexible, affordable, and widely applicable platform for diverse behavioral experiments.

title: Towards an Understanding of Context Utilization in Code Intelligence
authors: Yanlin Wang; Kefeng Duan; Dewu Zheng; Ensheng Shi; Fengji Zhang; Yanli Wang; Jiachi Chen; Xilin Liu; Yuchi Ma; Hongyu Zhang; Qianxiang Wang; Zibin Zheng
abstract: Code intelligence is an emerging domain in software engineering, aiming to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of various code-related tasks. Recent research suggests that incorporating contextual information beyond the basic original task inputs (i.e., source code) can substantially enhance model performance. Such contextual signals may be obtained directly or indirectly from sources such as API documentation or intermediate representations like abstract syntax trees can significantly improve the effectiveness of code intelligence. Despite growing academic interest, there is a lack of systematic analysis of context in code intelligence. To address this gap, we conduct an extensive literature review of 146 relevant studies published between September 2007 and August 2024. Our investigation yields four main contributions. (1) A quantitative analysis of the research landscape, including publication trends, venues, and the explored domains; (2) A novel taxonomy of context types used in code intelligence; (3) A task-oriented analysis investigating context integration strategies across diverse code intelligence tasks; (4) A critical evaluation of evaluation methodologies for context-aware methods. Based on these findings, we identify fundamental challenges in context utilization in current code intelligence systems and propose a research roadmap that outlines key opportunities for future research.

title: The Digital Sunset: Imagine Traditional Media Narratives About the Collapse of the Internet
authors: Giacomo Buoncompagni
abstract: This study explored the behaviour of traditional media in the event of an extreme situation: the global collapse of the internet, using a qualitative and creative methodology. The objective was not to make a technological prediction or engage in dystopian speculation, but rather to conduct a sociological experiment that interrogates the cultural, technical and symbolic presuppositions of contemporary communication. Using imagination as the primary methodological tool allowed a ‘potential’ case study to be constructed, while the use of artificial intelligence as an imaginative source and cognitive mediator enabled creativity and analytical rigour to be combined, producing a research space that bridges sociology, media science, and socio-technical studies (STS). The results demonstrate that, without the internet, analogue media emerge as essential social infrastructures, restoring journalism to its original purpose of facilitating human connections and collective narratives.
affiliations: University of Macerata

title: Understanding User Engagement with Cross-Platform Social Media Content Created by Humans Versus AI: An Evaluation of ChatGPT in Content Marketing
authors: Kholoud Aldous; Joni Salminen; Ali Farooq; Soon-gyo Jung; Bernard Jansen
abstract: Even though generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is increasingly integrated into user-facing technologies like social media, its impact on content marketing remains unverified. Early evidence suggests that language models (LLMs) can generate content that rivals human-created content (HCC) in terms of appeal. However, the question of adapting such content for various social media platforms remains unanswered. This study examines the effectiveness of an LLM, GPT-4, in customizing cross-platform content for Facebook, Instagram, and X. A total of 892 participants evaluated 30 pairs of AI-created content (ACC) and HCC. The findings reveal that ACC was preferred by users, delivered stronger calls to action, and elicited more user engagement than HCC, especially on Facebook, with a less pronounced effect for shorter posts on X and Instagram. We further generated six data-driven user personas of the 892 participants, illustrating the differences between those who preferred ACC or HCC on the three platforms. The results indicate that GPT-4 can adapt content to platform-specific requirements and maintain high perceived quality, making LLMs applicable for cross-platform content creation for user engagement. Findings contribute to understanding user engagement with AI-generated content across platforms. We also discuss the role of LLMs in content creation, including their ethical implications.
affiliations: Hamad bin Khalifa University; University of Vaasa; University of Strathclyde

title: Deepfake Technology Risks, Detection Methods, and Prevention Strategies
authors: Vaibhavi Gupta; Aditi Singh; Homa Rizvi; Dr. Yusuf Perwej
venue: International Journal of Scientific Research in Computer Science, Engineering and Information Technology
abstract: Deepfake technology, powered by advanced Artificial Intelligence and deep learning techniques, has rapidly evolved in recent years. While it offers innovative applications in entertainment, education, and digital media production, it also poses serious threats to security, privacy, and public trust. Deepfakes can be used to create highly realistic but manipulated audio, video, or images that may spread misinformation, damage reputations, enable fraud, or influence political and social environments. This paper examines the major threats associated with deepfake technology and highlights the potential risks it creates for individuals, organizations, and society. It further explores various techniques used for detecting deepfake content, including machine learning-based detection systems, forensic analysis methods, and AI-driven verification tools that analyze facial movements, voice patterns, and digital artifacts. In addition, the study discusses preventive measures such as digital watermarking, blockchain-based media verification, improved regulatory frameworks, and public awareness strategies to combat the misuse of deepfakes. The objective of this research is to provide a comprehensive understanding of deepfake threats while presenting effective detection and prevention mechanisms. By combining technological solutions with ethical guidelines and policy regulations, the study emphasizes the importance of developing reliable systems to maintain digital authenticity and protect information integrity in the modern digital ecosystem.
affiliations: Shri Ramswaroop Memorial University

title: What the Rise and Fall of Twitter Revealed about Collective Behaviour in Natural Hazards Disaster Response
authors: Leysia Palen
abstract: Introduction The golden age of social media revealed a great deal about the socio-behavioural phenomena of online activity during disaster events arising from natural hazards. The period from 2007 to 2012, when Twitter was new, provided insight into how members of the public—the informal response—naturally and collectively behaved when online social convergence was possible (Palen and Liu). For example, the now-pervasive “hashtag” was a user-driven invention made during the widespread 2007 Southern California Wildfires to enable the quick filtering of Twitter posts (Bruns and Burgess; Shklovski et al.). The practice stuck, and not only became built into Twitter’s features: it is now encoded across all forms of social media. Before problematic levels of disinforming behaviour plagued social media (Calo et al.), and before owner-executives monetised their platforms in ways that demoted time-sensitive information (Doctorow), social media spaces revealed the naturally-occurring socio-behavioural phenomena that arose in response to weather and geologic hazards. From intensive empirical social computational work, we uncovered a great deal about why digital connectivity is essential before and during disaster events (Palen et al., Vision; Sarcevic et al.; Hughes and Palen, Public Information Officer; Starbird and Palen, Disaster Desk; Kogan et al.; Bica et al., Hurricane Risks). In those mostly unadulterated circumstances at social media’s dawn, we found that the social convergence that occurred online mimicked what happened physically onsite (Stallings and Quarantelli): people asked for help, offered help, gathered to witness the event, and then, after these initialising interactions, sometimes worked together in improvisational ways to assist (Wachtendorf and Kendra). Initial basic informational exchange then gave rise to more complex, coordinated online work often between people who did not know one another (Kreps and Bosworth). In this way, social media platforms were incubators for innovative forms of collective action (Fritz and Mathewson), with “digital volunteers” warning and mobilising potential victims of disasters with timely information (Kaufhold). These socio-behavioural phenomena progressed across stages. Activities by members of the public drove early social media use, with formal emergency response joining later in response to this grassroots activity. Informal Responders Eyewitness Reporting. The first and most simple form of social media engagement were and continue to be reporting activities. Those who are proximate to a hazard provide eyewitness reports about flood levels, fire locations, traffic jams, power availability and so on. With social media originating around the same time smart phones became widely adopted in 2007, the mobility of social media applications combined with smart phone cameras led to dramatic change in collective behaviour (Palen and Hughes). Eyewitness reporting coupled with visual media became popularly referred to as citizen journalism (Vis), with the idea that citizens-as-sensors were a new way forward in early disaster response (Goodchild; Ottinger). The Convergence of Online Onlookers. Initial status reports were and continue to be the material around which observers then converge. The posts themselves, especially when accompanied by a hashtagged disaster term, created an online “site” for convergence within the vast information space of Twitter (Hughes et al., Site-Seeing). One opportunity for innovation was that the “data” produced by on-the-ground reporters could be collated for use by responders. Crucially, reports were automatically timestamped, meaning that the stream of reports could be tracked accurately in time. Crowdworkers, working quickly, manually collated reports, simulating what many hoped computational solutions could eventually do (Crowley and Chan; Ludwig et al., Crowdsourcing). However, because data came in the form of posts that relied on Twitter’s platform, software solutions were difficult to make a reality because they depended on the corporation’s platform configurations and policies that changed with some regularity. Another form of participation that arose from the influx of disaster reports references Sontag’s concerns about the photographic gaze upon the pain of others (Sontag): that of the “curious onlookers” who converged onto social media posts that depicted the devastation of disasters. Though this ability to bear witness contributed to monetary donations and, crucially, converted some observers into digital helpers, the reports could also enable exploitation, including in the form of “disaster tourism” and exploitation (Crawford and Finn; Elicessor). Fortunately, the worst forms of exploitation took some time to take hold in problematic ways, but as more people adopted social media and the scope of public interest extended beyond the local geographies of disasters, the possibility for exploitation of private information rose. People might publicly post addresses and phone numbers in desperate pleas for help, leaving them open for targeting. Today, disinforming tendencies indicate that exploitation of all kinds is possible (Starbird et al., Disinformation; Diamond et al.), which must inform how future civic computing solutions for disaster response might work. The Innovation of Online Help. However, in those early days, helpers who saw social media posts fly by were drawn to answer any array of questions: What roads were still open? Where could supplies be found? Who could assist in evacuating a family without a car? Often answers required rapid problem-solving with others who had supplemental information to be pieced together, such as making matches between requests for and offers of help. Remote helpers also acted as intermediaries who cast wide nets for gathering information. These behaviours became popularly known as distributed problem-solving that enabled the idealistic goal of collective intelligence (Meier). The idea was that people from any location could potentially assist. Those at a distance were suited to be collators of information that could be found by searching the Web, studying maps or making phone calls: a reliable Internet connection, electricity, and a quiet place to work were often missing for those proximate to a disaster (Crowley and Chan). During the 2009 Red River Flooding event, and long before the advent of pervasive bots that masquerade as humans today, a remote helper translated continuous river level data to publish as automated tweets—an idea that was highly innovative for the time (Starbird et al., Chatter). The Rise of the Digital Humanitarian. Some who understood the power of online help formed groups to specialise skill sets. This is known as self-organising (Comfort), and is common in the physical arena of disaster, and was expedited by Twitter in the digital arena (Reuter et al., Volunteers). Some self-organised groups evolved to the point of incorporating and creating brand identities that the formal response could interact with. The 2010 Haiti Earthquake marked the presence of organised digital volunteering, whose members were also known as digital humanitarians (Meier), and to a lesser but still notable extent, crisis tweeters and voluntweeters (Starbird and Palen, Voluntweeters), because of their dependence on Twitter for gathering and sharing intel. Their impact was palpable. Medical workers tweeted about medical needs on the ground and actively sought to coordinate with each other (Sarcevic et al.). Digital volunteers populated previously incomplete base maps provided by OpenStreetMap (Shaney et al.), an open geospatial database that professional humanitarian responders could use for navigation especially in parts of the world that are / were undermapped. This particular form of data became known as volunteered geographic information (VGI) (Haklay) and was the impetus for the incorporation of the Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team (HOT) (Soden and Palen). Other specialisations emerged. Volunteers who eventually seeded the volunteer organisations of Humanity Road and the Standby Task Force (SBTF) (Norris) attempted to help Haitians, including, for example, by amassing donations to buy minutes for their mobile phones, which also required collating those phone numbers through trusted contacts. Some of the network of people with financially supported phones in turn became trusted reporters of on-the-ground information. Though the toolsets extended beyond Twitter to include other social media and open data platforms (Shanley et al.), Twitter was essential in soliciting volunteers and donations, and communicating broadly the impact of their work (Reuter; Starbird and Palen, Disaster Desk). Organised helpers took their experiences from the Haiti earthquake response to develop mission statements, establish policies, practices, and divisions of labour, and to create training and onboarding programs to assist with subsequent disasters around the world. They sought to work with local formal response efforts to be tasked with specific information-based work (Starbird and Palen, Voluntweeters). The US Forest Service took these emerging forms of online work a step further: its employees established VOSTs in 2011—Virtual Operations Support Teams—which could be assembled when large wildfires begin (St. Denis and Palen; Silver et al.). However, the US Forest Service sought experienced wildfire responders who volunteered to act as intermediaries between fire fighters active on the ground and the attending social media audience. In this way, VOSTs represented the first step in the incorporation of the digital humanitarianism that was happening in the public sphere into the professional world of emergency response and has also been trialled in other parts of the world, where its use seems to have endured beyond the initial US case (Fathi et al.). The Evolution of Practice in Digital Assistance. The practice of digital volunteerism needed several cycles of disasters to develop as social media opportunities and harms were being discovered. Hazards had different information demands as well, and the nature of what to expect online changed as more people signed onto Twitter. For example, a first-generation tenet of digital volunteerism was to amplify tweets that asked for help. To amplify was to retweet until messages got the attention of people on the ground, or of the formal response. The other point of the amplification of tweets was to assure that messages did not get lost in a long stream of tweets before notice could be taken. Eventually, it became clear that not only was amplification too passive a way to help, it could also be harmful. Over-amplified messages would saturate the information space even after going out of date. Even more importantly, amplification was pushing some messaging higher in priority as a matter of luck, which became problematic for emergency responders who felt pressure to allocate resources differently from what they thought best (Burns and Shanley). Critiques of the idealised hopes of social media arose as well, in tandem with innovation. Scholars questioned assumptions about how well Twitter posts could represent reality, directing concerns about how much machine learning innovations and journalism should rely on its streams as a source (Crawford and Finn; Lewis and Molyneux). Those who can post do post, and the absence of information might be as telling as its presence. Emergency responders shared this concern and adapted social media integration into their practice in their own ways. Amplification was eventually replaced by the collation and tracking of messages, which was the tipping point for more sophisticated and coordinated forms of help. This is where the Twitter timestamp became essential for determining when a message originated and what actions might have been taken in the time since. The appearance of posts in sequential order was therefore essential to coordinated, self-organising disaster response work across helpers for some objective measure of new and old information (Norris). Twitter posts provided the material, but digital humanitarian teams used other tools to collaboratively track incoming information and team chat solutions to coordinate in real-time. Once Twitter introduced algorithmic preferences to reorder how posts were presented to different users, confidence in content tracking and the felicity of the coordinated work took a nosedive (Norris). Observing the disaster through the window of Twitter degraded informationally, but no other platform could sufficiently take its place. Though those working in the emergency response social media space grew to understand the limitations of its ability to represent a disaster event “accurately” across the geography it affected, having an unreliable stream of what was known to be partial data was all the worse. Suddenly, corporate algorithmic decisions made it very hard to insert human discernment into the calculus about what constituted a picture of the disaster. In personal communications with Twitter executives in 2015, it was clear that they did not appreciate the critical dependence digital humanitarians had on the sequential delivery of posts, assuming that API calls could compensate for the change, here again failing to realise that even free API access was a barrier to use among low-resourced volunteers. The place of Twitter as part of a growing disaster infrastructure (Finn) was a low priority, and the atemporality and invisible mechanisms of amplification of posts effectively scrambled any existing signal. The early effects of “enshittification” (Doctorow) were sorely felt. Another evolution of practice came about when the volunteer digital humanitarians attempted to respond the world over. They realised that a core, experienced team needed to be in place to launch an effective digital response, but that onboarding new and local volunteers who could sustain the effort around the clock and employ localised knowledge was essential. Though the idea of a flexible organisation was a good one, it was hard to put into practice. New volunteers needed to be constantly trained, and people working across time zones often had difficulty with temporal coordination (Norris et al.). In addition, each disaster brought new waves of helper-onlookers who were limited by local knowledge. Research conducted in the aftermath of the 2012 Hurricane Sandy and the 2015 Nepal Earthquakes showed that locals made an effort to follow and help locals (Kogan et al.); they also tweeted photos that provided original logistical content such as information about road passage and shelter status (Bica et al.). Those helpers at a distance, however, in particular with the much later Nepal Earthquake, retweeted dramatic photos of injured victims and heavy destruction, sometimes even from other, past earthquakes in other locations (Bica et al.). Though their efforts might have increased donations world-wide, the visuals called to mind concerns about exploitation (Sontag), fair representation (Crawford and Finn), and how those curated visuals affected our very experience of disaster (Finn). The Formal Response In early disaster events, emergency management by and large was not attuned to the sudden influx of Twitter activity. Eventually, however, in many countries, the responsibility of social media engagement largely fell to public information officers. In speaking to emergency management audiences in those early days, reactions to the inevitable social media wave varied. Some were aware of the impending boom but wondered how to engage with it. Problematically, government workers in many countries (e.g., Ludwig et al., Haystack) were prohibited from having applications like Twitter on their work-provided devices, which was a significant hurdle initially for participation. Others felt that public participation was dangerous. The reckoning with the radical change in crisis communications was palpable. In addition, substantial attention was paid to the matter of the veracity of posts: were the statements true? Could sources be trusted? Though these were legitimate concerns even in those early days when information disorder could be managed manually, this hyperfocus on truth led to trepidation from emergency managers about interacting with Twitter at all (Palen and Anderson). Adaptation to the inevitable—that people were and would continue to turn to social media during disasters—was the first lesson, and the formal response cautiously came around as public demand grew and as information was being shared peer-to-peer without involvement by emergency management. Other concerns about danger to the public that formal responders raised were also instructive. If public information officers were to publicly inquire over Twitter about, for example, flooding threat or road damage, this could inadvertently compel people to go to areas that would put them at risk. Attending to online calls for help could mean improperly prioritising resources. Liability became a driving legal and policy concern both for emergency managers as well as the “Good Samaritans” who volunteered to help (Burns and Shanley; Robson). Given that “harvesting” of data (Castillo) from reports created problems for emergency response (Zade et al.), formal responders engaged in their own innovations to incorporate Twitter into their work. Recall the VOST initiative, which employed fire responders as trusted volunteers to relay distilled content back to incident command. Other solutions included making sure that formal readouts included Twitter as a place for announcements. When announcements could not be publicly made, information officers would quietly monitor social media posts and intercede to mitigate erroneous information. This way, good information from unofficial sources remained available, even though emergency responders could not officially comment on it. They cleverly engendered a protective role of the information space while also minding social media policies in place at the time (personal communication). Eventually, emergency management did find official and stable ways to engage. In the 2012 Hurricane Sandy event that struck the US eastern seaboard including New York City, about 70% of police and fire departments in the region posted on their Facebook accounts and nearly all using their standing Websites, indicating that having a stable “front door” onto the Internet was important to them. Engagement on Twitter was far less, at 10-20% having active accounts during that event (Hughes et al.). The simple matter of having limited staff to manage the herculean task limited social media engagement. We suspect that the low number of departments using Twitter combined with the high number of the same departments using other media came about because @FDNY (the Fire Department of New York) did a strong job of representing all. Even though the disaster was well beyond the scope of New York City, the steady participation of a few strong Twitter accounts from emergency response supported the rest. As time went on, emergency managers around the world showed an understanding that Twitter-enabled listening was important (Denef et al.). Indeed, as more emergency representatives came online, they attracted many followers. Weather forecasters became notable trusted authoritative sources around whom people would gather during hurricanes and floods (Bica et al., Hurricane Risks). It was clear that, in the vast world of Twitter, being able to find authoritative voices was important to members of the public: they would rely on unofficial sources if those were the only active voices, but they clustered around official voices when possible. Eventually, local emergency management groups came to strongly rely on Twitter as well as other social media platforms to message constituents. New significant problems then emerged. First, the volume of Twitter activity was so vast that they needed help with listening and sorting. Multiple research efforts tried to help with this (Anderson et al., Architectural; Anderson et al., Scalable; Gupta et al.; Ludwig et al., Haystack), but the exponential growth of social media activity and the technical changes corporations made to their platforms made it difficult for tools to sit atop these shifting commercial products. In addition, after emergency responders had devoted limited resources to building up a steady audience of constituents to whom they could reliably communicate out, the same algorithmic changes to Twitter that affected participation by informal responders meant that messages from the formal responders were often hidden. This became a major problem for emergency managers. What’s more, it was a major indicator of just how much emergency management came to depend on social media tools to communicate to constituents rapidly, flexibly, and interactively. They had come a long way from those initial years of reticence. The power of targeted crisis communication was finally realised—but was problematically at the mercy of corporate whims whose platforms served many purposes. The Dissolution of Reliable Crisis Communication The eroding reliability of crisis communication over Twitter was not helped by the COVID-19 pandemic that began in 2020, at which time concerns about public health gave way to new levels of controversy. Though public health crises are considered natural hazard disasters, the hazardous agent of the COVID-19 virus was embodied in people and therefore anthropomorphised. In comparison to a hazard like a hurricane—a shared experience of an exogenous agent from which all must retreat—this world-wide viral hazard engendered mistrust between people. Social media activity in the absence of sufficient early knowledge about how to truly protect oneself focussed on “flattening the curve” of how many people would be affected; interpersonal judgement arose around how protectively or not people behaved. Differing opinions about how nation-states should respond to the pandemic rendered the response highly political. Furthermore, the world-wide experience of lockdowns meant that social media communications by and large were inflected with pandemic concerns no matter what other topics they might have also been about. The world of Twitter was consumed by crisis communications of a kind that stood apart from the classic warning- and help-based messaging we had seen during geological and weather hazards. In addition, anti-vaccine advocates repurposed pandemic-related messages to cause unfounded fear among marginalised populations about receiving COVID-19 vaccines, reaching its height in 2021 when vaccines became widely available (Diamond et al.; Palen et al., Fertile Ground). Furthermore, it is important to note that Donald Trump during the first year of the pandemic had a Twitter follower base that was unmatched by anyone worldwide (88m)—followers who ardently retweeted his frequent messages (an average of 33 tweets per day), leading to an outsized international informational footprint that made finding signal impossibly difficult even with sophisticated search functionality (Palen et al., Medium). The once manageable public square was gone, and crisis communications were deeply entangled in a range of other issues, and often highly disordered. When Twitter was acquired by tech billionaire Elon Musk a year later in 2022, all access to APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) was soon removed, and important internal groups that strove to limit dangerous and offensive content were eliminated. Musk eventually renamed the platform X, setting the path for making this highly popular microblogging platform his own personal political propaganda machine. People left in droves. Others, now further unbridled behaviourally, disregarded any remaining attempts at maintaining the civic nature of the public sphere. With few other places that possessed a critical mass (Markus) of listeners, emergency managers stayed on X in an attempt to communicate outward to constituents, but their reach was dramatically limited. The disaster infrastructure (Finn) that had grown to include Twitter, and that so many relied on, as imperfect as it was in its early days, was no more. Few alternative solutions appear to have arisen in its place, with Watch Duty, a non-profit application-based platform dedicated to wildfire watch in the US American West, serving as an exception. It is notable that many of the approaches of Watch Duty echo the best of what we might now see as the golden era of early social media behaviours by informal and formal emergency response. Public Media Are Essential to the Future of Emergency Response It is important to understand that the infrastructure we grew to depend on was effectively experimental. Twitter was one of the earliest training grounds for microblogging. It was built because it could be, with an underspecified set of features that allowed for human interaction to shape its use, at least initially. It became a one-stop shop for all topics: it was all-purpose, not purpose-built. In terms of crisis response, this does have an advantage at least initially. It had the benefit of being a place with a critical mass of users. People were already there, chatting about other topics, and could switch to crisis communications should they arise, creating what Hermida described as a “nervous system for the planet” (117). Even occasional users knew they could go there and probably find the information they needed in disaster situations. It was expected that information that might appear elsewhere on broadcast media, or elsewhere online, would likely eventually traverse through Twitter. Even if one person was not a user, a neighbour or family member would be. This seemed sufficient for a saturation spread of information in disaster response. In this way, the all-purpose quality of the space was useful—until the geographical and population reach became too vast, at which point it began to buckle under its own weight even before more nefarious behaviours took over. Corporate interests finished it off, with algorithmic priorities (Doctorow) rendering Twitter less useful for disaster response, including for emergency managers who were no longer certain they were reaching their constituents, and for the digital humanitarians for whom sequentially timestamped data were the grist for their mill. Their work was troubled by this change. The Musk takeover finished it off, though emergency managers still use X in an attempt to push communications out. The digital volunteer humanitarian effort is largely quiet, except for the carefully managed versions of it offered by Watch Duty for wildfire hazards. It remains to be seen how emergency response will reorganise its digital communications. Though Twitter had its critics early on, the loss of it has revealed valuable characteristics for the future of public media. Twitter’s commitment to supplying robust APIs for a long time without significant fees allowed software engineers to help emergency response to pull posts fairly efficiently, though the constantly changing platform introduced a great deal of instability in letting non-commercial solutions grow. Twitter allowed academics to study the activities of people online as they were developing new information behaviours (Castillo), acting as watchdogs who could sound the alarm when troublesome activity was arising (Starbird et al., Boston). Now, few social media applications offer this level of API support. Social media platforms that exist are, technically speaking, places of public gathering but whose activities are not truly visible to anyone other than the corporate interests that enable them. This loss of crisis communication is tragic, and the disordered information ecosystem that we are now living with is a disaster all its own. The Way Forward: Interactive Public Media in Emergency Management The absence of reliable infrastructure for digital public communications shows just how essential it has become. We have learned that peer-to-peer and informal-to-formal communication is expected, and that members of the public want to and will help, if only we can protect the information ecosystem in which these exchanges can happen. No longer does the public wait for crisis communication only from authoritative sources; they expect the benefits of engaged interaction between members of the public and the formal response. In addition, though it was volunteerism that paved the way for innovative new forms of information behaviours, we must promote new forms of disaster informational work to professional positions. The lesson is not to rely so strongly on volunteers, but rather to attend to what creative solutions they enabled in their desire to help. It also has become painfully clear that we cannot depend on corporate solutions that need to monetise communications and limit perceived liability. Nonprofit public media that are purpose-built for emergency response seems to be a more predictable route to go, though how these are managed and the degree to which trust can be engendered by either non-government or government-led organisations will vary around the world. Interactive public media need to be imagined and housed in local government contexts that also mind the need for international agreements, as natural hazards do not obey human-made boundaries. For these reasons, solutions around the world will differ. Ideally, the “best of” of such implementations that enable safe, secure, and reliable interactions between the informal and formal responses can be shared, perhaps through trusted international partnerships of emergency management. Careful design and deployment are necessary to ensure that a critical mass of people attend to these sources to make them viable. Equally problematic is over-warning constituents if an all-incidents approach is taken in the design: the practice of high-density warnings in South Korea is a lesson in oversaturating audiences. Solutions will also need to offer stable data representations so that computational solutions built on top of them are themselves stable. It is clear that solutions cannot come from a few Silicon Valley technocrats whose ill-directed ideations are the basis on which crisis communications infrastructure has been built in response to fast-moving technological development. I would argue that the biggest lesson of all is that it is time for the expertise of emergency management to itself lead this cause, drawing upon its expert practice, and on the valuable lessons learned from the early experimental days of Twitter. title: SOCIAL MEDIA POST & NEWS SUMMERIZER & AUTHENTICITY VERIFICATION
authors: Anshul Anshul; Charan Pote; Pranay Tembhurne; Lokesh Kadawe; Pratham Bawankar; Sahil Hatwar
venue: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH IN ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
abstract: Abstract—A Social Media Post and News Summarizer with Authenticity Verification is an intelligent web-based system designed to address the growing challenge of misinformation in today’s digital ecosystem. With the exponential increase in online content generated through news platforms and social media, users are frequently exposed to vast amounts of information that may be incomplete, biased, or misleading. The rapid spread of such content creates significant challenges in identifying reliable information and increases the risk of misinformation influencing public perception and decision-making. Existing systems primarily focus on either automatic content summarization or fake news detection independently, lacking a unified approach that ensures both clarity and credibility. To overcome this limitation, the proposed system integrates advanced Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to perform automated summarization and authenticity verification within a single framework. The system collects data from multiple trusted sources, preprocesses it to remove noise and redundancy, and generates concise and meaningful summaries using AI-based models. In addition to summarization, the system incorporates a multi-layered authenticity verification mechanism that evaluates information reliability using parameters such as Credibility Score, Agreement Score, and Confidence Level. The Credibility Score assesses the trustworthiness of sources, the Agreement Score measures consistency across multiple sources, and the Confidence Level provides an overall reliability indicator. This structured evaluation enables users to quickly understand not only the content but also its level of trustworthiness. The platform is developed using a modern and scalable architecture, ensuring efficient data processing and real-time response. It supports seamless user interaction, allowing users to input news articles or social media posts and instantly receive summarized content along with authenticity metrics. The transparent scoring mechanism enhances user trust by clearly indicating how the evaluation is performed. Furthermore, the system significantly reduces information overload by presenting only essential and verified information, thereby saving time and improving user comprehension. By combining AI-driven summarization with multi-source verification, the proposed system enhances digital literacy, promotes critical thinking, and supports informed decision-making. Furthermore, the system significantly reduces information overload by presenting only essential and verified information, thereby saving time and improving user comprehension. By combining AI-driven summarization with multi-source verification, the proposed system enhances digital literacy, promotes critical thinking, and supports informed decision-making. Overall, this system represents a significant advancement toward building a reliable and transparent information ecosystem, where users can access concise, accurate, and trustworthy information in real time. The proposed approach not only addresses current challenges in misinformation detection but also provides a scalable foundation for future enhancements in intelligent information processing systems.

title: CRYPTOVISTA CRYPTOCURRENCY TRADING PLATFORM
authors: Gaurav Kokane; Dr. Pratibha V. Kashid; Prathamesh Pandit; Hitesh Patil; Prathamesh Chormale
venue: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH IN ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
abstract: ABSTRACT Cryptocurrency trading has rapidly evolved into a highly dynamic and technology-driven financial domain, attracting significant attention from investors, researchers, and institutions worldwide. This paper presents a comprehensive review of modern cryptocurrency trading platforms by combining blockchain technology, artificial intelligence, and advanced trading mechanisms to create a secure, efficient, and scalable trading ecosystem. The study highlights the use of deep learning approaches such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and attention-based models for improving cryptocurrency price prediction. These techniques utilize technical indicators, trading patterns, and social media data to enhance prediction accuracy. In addition, reinforcement learning strategies are explored to optimize trading decisions and improve performance under highly volatile market conditions. Furthermore, the paper discusses real-time data integration using APIs, secure authentication mechanisms, and scalable system architectures required for continuous trading operations. It also examines the regulatory landscape of cryptocurrency, particularly in the Indian context, including taxation policies and emerging concepts like Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs). Overall, this review provides insights into the development of intelligent, secure, and user-friendly cryptocurrency trading platforms such as CryptoVista. Keywords: Cryptocurrency, Blockchain, Deep Learning, Reinforcement Learning, Smart Contracts, Real-Time Data, Trading Platforms, Security, Scalability, CBDC
affiliations: Visvesvaraya National Institute of Technology

title: HOW TECHNOLOGY IS CHANGING THE WAY WE USE ENGLISH
authors: Jaloldinov Islomiddin Shamsitdin o'g'li; Dadaboyeva Nodirabonu Abdulhakim qizi
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
abstract: Abstract: The rapid advancement of digital technology has fundamentally transformed the English language, altering its vocabulary, syntax, and modes of delivery. This comprehensive article explores the multifaceted impact of technology on English, particularly focusing on the emergence of "Netspeak," the proliferation of acronyms and emojis, the role of social media in language learning, and the profound influence of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on academic writing. Designed for university students, this paper synthesizes recent sociolinguistic data from 2025 and 2026 to demonstrate how digital communication is not degrading standard English, but rather expanding its repertoire. Furthermore, it addresses critical challenges faced by English Language Learners (ELLs), such as AI detection bias. Ultimately, this study argues that technological integration into language is a natural evolutionary process that requires modern learners to develop robust digital and linguistic fluency. Keywords: Digital Linguistics, Netspeak, Artificial Intelligence, English Language Learners (ELLs), Social Media, Emojis, Academic Writing. Introduction. Language is a living, breathing entity that constantly evolves to meet the communicative needs of its users. In the 21st century, the most significant catalyst for this evolution is digital technology. The advent of the internet, smartphones, social media platforms, and Artificial Intelligence (AI) has revolutionized not only how we communicate but also the very structure and vocabulary of the English language. English, serving as the dominant lingua franca of the digital world, is at the epicenter of this linguistic metamorphosis. For university students today, navigating the English language means mastering multiple registers. A student must know how to draft a formal academic essay using standard syntax while simultaneously understanding the nuanced, fast-paced, and highly abbreviated language of text messaging and social media. The English linguist David Crystal advocated for the domain of "Internet Linguistics" to study these new language styles and forms. As we progress through the 2020s, the transition from simple text-based expressions to a multimodal language incorporating video, GIFs, and AI-generated text has become complete. This article aims to provide a comprehensive overview of how technology is reshaping English. It will explore the rise of internet slang and abbreviations, the psychological and communicative role of emojis, the utilization of social media as an informal learning ecosystem, and the dual-edged sword of AI in academic writing. By understanding these technological impacts, students can better equip themselves to communicate effectively and appropriately across all digital and physical platforms. The Evolution of Netspeak and Digital Slang. The term "Netspeak" refers to the distinct type of language used on the internet. Initially characterized by simple text-based expressions in the 1990s, Netspeak has transitioned into a highly complex, multimodal language by the mid-2020s. The technical limitations of early text messages (such as character limits on SMS and early Twitter) resulted in the reinvention of words and the construction of a new language style. Today, however, the driving force behind Netspeak is not character limitation, but cultural identity, speed, and the desire for linguistic economy. Generation Z and Generation Alpha have been particularly instrumental in driving language change. Social media platforms like TikTok, Instagram, and X (formerly Twitter) serve as incubators for new vocabulary. Words and phrases are coined, popularized globally within days, and sometimes discarded just as quickly. A 2025 sociolinguistic analysis of TikTok discourse notes that slang and informal expressions commonly appear as markers of group identity and social alignment. Terms like "crashing out" (losing control or acting recklessly), "locking in" (focusing intensely), and "delulu" (delusional) have become staples of modern youth communication. Interestingly, older generations often view this rapid influx of slang as a degradation of the English language. Critics argue that ";Netspeak" and "Leetspeak" are destroying literacy. However, linguists argue the opposite: internet slang is not ruining communication skills; it simply reflects how language adapts to new environments. It requires a high degree of linguistic dexterity to switch between the informal "brain rot" vernacular of TikTok and the formal requirements of a university research paper. Slang Term Definition / Meaning Context of Use Delulu Short for delusional; holding onto unrealistic beliefs. Often used humorously in romantic contexts ("stay delulu"). Lore The backstory or history of a person, relationship, or event. "I need to catch up on the lore of this friend group." Locking in Focusing intensely on a task, such as studying or working. "Finals are tomorrow, I'm locking in tonight." Slay To do something exceptionally well; to succeed. Used as a term of high praise or encouragement. No cap Being completely honest; not lying. Used to emphasize the truthfulness of a statement. As demonstrated in Table 1, these terms are highly contextual. For English Language Learners (ELLs), mastering this digital vocabulary is often just as important for social integration as mastering standard grammar is for academic success. The digital linguascape is a dynamic environment where linguistic impoliteness, humor, and creativity intersect, fundamentally reshaping the English lexicon. Abbreviations and Acronyms: The Need for Speed. Alongside slang, the proliferation of acronyms and abbreviations is a defining characteristic of digital English. In a fast-paced digital world, efficiency is paramount. A study conducted at Jadara University in 2025 investigating the use of acronyms among undergraduate students revealed a high prevalence of abbreviation use across platforms, particularly on WhatsApp and Snapchat. The most common included universal internet acronyms like "LOL" (Laughing Out Loud), "BRB" (Be Right Back), and "ASAP" (As Soon As Possible). The motivations behind using these abbreviations are multifaceted. Primarily, they serve the purpose of linguistic economy—saving time and keystrokes. However, they also serve socio-cultural functions, such as expressing informality, establishing a relaxed tone, and signaling membership in a specific digital community. Despite their widespread use, abbreviations can sometimes hinder communication. A 2025 study found that most Americans still struggle to understand newer text acronyms like "AYOR" (At Your Own Risk) or "RLRT" (Real Life Retweet), exposing a growing gap in digital fluency. Figure 1: The evolution of the English language in the digital age, highlighting the impact of technology, AI, and digital linguistics trends. Furthermore, the psychological impact of abbreviations is complex. A November 2024 study published by the American Psychological Association (APA) revealed that while 99% of respondents reported using texting abbreviations, using them can sometimes make the sender appear insincere or less committed to the conversation. In professional or academic contexts, over-reliance on abbreviations can detract from the perceived professionalism of the message. Therefore, students must develop "pragmatic competence"—the ability to know when it is appropriate to use "textese" and when standard English is required. Research into the impact of ";textese" on children's grammar has yielded surprising results. While many assume texting degrades grammar, studies (such as those by van Dijk et al.) have shown that the use of textese can actually be positively related to grammar performance. The ability to manipulate language, omit words strategically, and use textisms requires a solid underlying understanding of how the language works. It is a form of code-switching, akin to bilingualism, where the user switches between formal written English and digital shorthand. Emojis: The New Digital Non-Verbal Communication In face-to-face communication, meaning is conveyed not just through words, but through tone of voice, facial expressions, and body language. In text-based digital communication, these crucial non-verbal cues are stripped away, often leaving messages open to misinterpretation. To fill this void, technology gave us the emoji. Emojis have quietly become the emotional punctuation of modern communication. According to 2025 statistics, an estimated 10 billion emojis are sent every day worldwide. The Unicode Standard now includes nearly 4,000 official emojis. They are no longer just decorative symbols; they function as a digital language with their own dialects. The Social Information Processing Theory suggests that people use available behavioral cues to develop relationships online. Emojis serve this exact function, enriching "less rich" text exchanges by conveying emotional nuance, increasing expressiveness, and fostering relationship development. In the workplace and academic settings, emojis are increasingly accepted. An Adobe trend report highlighted that 73% of people feel emojis make them seem cooler, friendlier, and funnier. Furthermore, 62% of professionals believe emojis can boost team morale. However, interpretation varies wildly across generations. While Generation Z might use the skull emoji (💀) to indicate they are "dead"; from laughter, older generations might interpret it literally or find it morbid. Similarly, the simple thumbs-up (👍) is viewed as a standard acknowledgment by Millennials and Gen X, but is often perceived as passive-aggressive or dismissive by Gen Z. title: Combating Digital Deception: A Taxonomical and Systematic Review of Deepfake Detection Approaches
authors: Pratishtha Gupta; Bhawna Narwal; Amar Kumar Mohapatra
venue: WIREs Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery
abstract: ABSTRACT The contemporary world is fuelled by the social web, with approximately 504.17 h of video content and 1,050,000 photos going up online every minute. This open‐access media is swiftly exploited to facilitate deepfake generation that can disseminate misleading information and tarnish reputations. Furthermore, the subtlety of deepfakes has escalated to the point where deep learning‐based techniques yield deepfakes that are perceptibly flawless and indistinguishable from reality. Moreover, the existing detection models suffer severe affliction when in the crosshairs of adversarial attacks. Therefore, it is paramount to promote the development of resilient evasion strategies against this societal bane, which “takes the bull by the horns” while shielding them from hostile adversaries. In this direction, the presented article outlines the neoteric progression in deepfake topography, assimilating generation, detection, and adversarial attack‐based techniques. It delineates an all‐inclusive taxonomy based on topical research stemming from the deepfake landscape. Based on the taxonomical classification, a systematic and exhaustive review of the avant‐garde deepfake detection techniques is conducted. Furthermore, this article reviews state‐of‐the‐art active defense techniques against audio/video deepfakes, incorporating both generator‐ and detector‐based adversarial attacks, and discusses issues with deepfake datasets. Lastly, the trends in deepfake detection and dataset utilization have been highlighted, bringing to the fore the all‐important spheres requiring futuristic investigation. This article is categorized under: Technologies > Artificial Intelligence Technologies > Machine Learning Commercial, Legal, and Ethical Issues > Security and Privacy

title: Cybersecurity in Action
authors: Somayye Nasiri; Shahram Shahabi; Aynaz Shafiesabet; Maral Talebbeidokhti; Elmira Alvandi Behineh
abstract: This research aimed to develop and assess a framework for exploring how factors at the organizational, social, and individual levels influence cybersecurity behavior (CB), with a particular focus on the intermediary role played by awareness of cybersecurity. Participants in this investigation included 381 employees from various public sector entities, with data being gathered via a questionnaire. The analysis was conducted through SEM, specifically employing the PLS. Outputs from this study indicate that cybersecurity awareness (CA), security self-efficacy (SSE), the perceived threat severity, and the perceived vulnerability all positively and significantly impact CBs. Additionally, the research found that initiatives focused on security training and awareness, information security culture, social media influence, family and friends influence, and information systems knowledge all significantly contribute to enhancing CA. Similarly, these elements, alongside direct experience in information security, were shown to affec SSE, with the exception of the influence exerted by family and friends, which did not show a significant impact on it. Influences from social media, family and friends, and direct experiences with information security were also found to significantly affect the perceived severity of threats and perceived of vulnerability. Thus, this study concludes that the factors analyzed are strong indicators of behavior related to cybersecurity.
